papers.title: On the Formation of Runaway Stars BN and x in the Orion Nebula Cluster
papers.author_names: Juan P. Farias Jonathan C. Tan

abstract.text: We explore scenarios for the dynamical ejection of stars BN and x from source I in the Kleinmann-Low nebula of the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC), which is important for being the closest region of massive star formation. This ejection would cause source I to become a close binary or a merger product of two stars. We thus consider binary-binary encounters as the mechanism to produce this event. By running a large suite of $N$-body simulations, we find that it is nearly impossible to match the observations when using the commonly adopted masses for the participants, especially a source I mass of $7\\\\:{\\\\rm{M}}_\\\\odot$. The only way to recreate the event is if source I is more massive, i.e., $\\\\sim20\\\\:{\\\\rm{M}}_\\\\odot$. However, even in this case, the likelihood of reproducing the observed system is low. We discuss the implications of these results for understanding this important star-forming region.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A large-scale $N$-body simulation study reveals that dynamical ejection of runaway stars BN and x from the Orion Nebula Cluster's source I is only feasible if source I has a mass of $\sim 20\,M_\odot$, contradicting standard $7\,M_\odot$ estimates. The simulations show that binary-binary encounters fail to reproduce observed kinematics under conventional masses, implying either a massive source I or a non-standard interaction mechanism, challenging current models of massive star formation and dynamical ejection in dense clusters.

source_fields.title: On the Formation of Runaway Stars BN and x in the Orion Nebula Cluster

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a dynamical ejection scenario in which a binary-binary interaction (Case 1) leads to the ejection of BN and x from a four-star system, with the core idea being that close dynamical encounters between two initially bound binaries can produce observed stellar velocities within 2σ of measurements, and that such ejections are most likely when the initial system consists of two interacting binaries rather than more complex configurations. Results: The BNx-ejection interactions have significantly higher cross sections and branching ratios (up to 39.4% and 35.0% in Case A) compared to BNx-velocity interactions (up to 13.7% and 10.4% in Case B), with the former dominating for larger primary masses and circular eccentricities, while the latter shows negligible rates for high-mass systems. Introduction: This paper presents a novel set of $10^7$ $N$-body simulations exploring a binary-binary interaction scenario in which a binary protostar system (I₁–I₂) dynamically interacts with a BN–x binary, leading to the ejection of BN and source x and resolving the mass discrepancy in momentum conservation while providing a physically plausible mechanism for the runaway motion of BN and source I. Discussion and Conclusions: Simulations show that the observed velocities of the BN and x system are only achievable if source I has a mass of at least $20\\>{\\rm{M}}_{\\odot}$, with momentum analysis confirming that inclusion of source x weakens but does not eliminate the need for a high mass, supporting a massive progenitor rather than a low-mass one. Appendix A Branching ratios for all the possible outcomes: The branching ratios show that the "2 Singles + Binary" outcome, particularly with a massive source I, is relatively common and can produce observed velocities, though only with increased stellar masses, and no such match is found under the original mass assumptions.

title: On the Formation of Runaway Stars BN and x in the Orion Nebula Cluster

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical N-body simulation study exploring ejection mechanisms for two specific stars; no new observational data presented or catalog compiled
